title: STA258
text:
The Big Question

How can we say something true aboutmillions of people we’ve never met?




Pollsters call ~1,000 Canadians and predict an election for 40 million. Doctors test a drug on 500 patients and recommend it for everyone. Netflix watches a sample of users and decides what shows to make.




That gap between “what we measured” and “what we want to know” - that’s what this whole course is about.

title: STA258
text:
Roadmap


1.1 Foundations

Statistics, population, sample
Unit, observation, variable
Parameter vs statistic
Descriptive vs inferential


1.2 Types of Data

Quantitative vs qualitative
Discrete vs continuous
Nominal vs ordinal

1.3 Inference Preview

Sampling, bias, study types

title: STA258
text:
1.1 Foundations

Definition 1.1 - Statistics The science of collecting, classifying, summarizing, analyzing, and interpreting data.




Think of statistics as a pipeline:  Question → Data → Analysis → Decision


The five verbs in the definition map roughly to the chapters of the course.

title: STA258
text:
Population

Definition 1.2 - Population The entire group of individuals, items, or measurements that share a characteristic of interest and about which conclusions are to be drawn.




Examples:

All students at the University of Toronto
All residents of Mississauga
Every possible hand in a game of poker (hypothetical!)
Every battery a factory will ever produce





A population can be real or hypothetical, finite or essentially infinite. What matters is that it’s the group you actually want to learn about.

title: STA258
text:
Sample

Definition 1.3 - Sample A subset selected from the population.



Examples:

50 randomly selected U of T students
100 randomly selected Mississauga residents
30 batteries pulled off the production line today




Why sample? Measuring everyone is usually too slow, too expensive, or simply impossible.

title: STA258
text:
Visualizing Population vs Sample







Hover over any dot to see which unit it is. Grey = population, teal = the sample.

title: STA258
text:
Your Turn: Identify Them

A researcher wants to know the average sleep of all UTM students. She randomly surveys 200 UTM students and finds they sleep 6.8 hours on average.  What is the population? What is the sample?



Population: All UTM students. Sample: The 200 surveyed UTM students.


Pause and have students answer aloud before revealing.

title: STA258
text:
Unit, Observation, Variable

Unit - the smallest entity from which data are collected (one row).


Observation - the full set of measured values for one unit.


Variable - a characteristic that varies from unit to unit (one column).

title: STA258
text:
Reading a Data Table



Student
Age
Program
Study Hours
On Campus




1
19
Statistics
20
Yes


2
22
Computer Science
15
No


3
20
Biology
28
Yes


4
21
Psychology
18
No






Each row is one observation (one student)
Each column is one variable
The unit is a single student

title: STA258
text:
Try It in R









Run it. Then change a value or add another student.

title: STA258
text:
Population Unit vs Sample Unit



Population unit One member of the entire population.


One U of T student, when the population is all U of T students.



Sample unit One member of the selected sample.


One of the 50 students you actually surveyed.




Every sample unit is also a population unit - but not the other way around.

title: STA258
text:
Parameter vs Statistic



Parameter A number that describes the population. Usually unknown.



Statistic A number that describes the sample. Calculated from data - known.




Memory trick:   Parameter ↔︎ Population  |  Statistic ↔︎ Sample  (both start with the same letter).

title: STA258
text:
Notation You Need to Memorize



Quantity
Population (parameter)
Sample (statistic)




Mean
\(\mu\)
\(\bar{x}\)


Standard deviation
\(\sigma\)
\(s\)


Proportion
\(p\)
\(\hat{p}\)


Size
\(N\)
\(n\)





Greek letters → population.   Roman letters (or hats) → sample.


Stress this. Most algebra errors later in the course come from mixing \(p\) with \(\hat{p}\) or \(\sigma\) with \(s\).

title: STA258
text:
Quick Check

For each, decide: parameter or statistic?


The average height of all NBA players.


63% of 500 surveyed Canadians support a policy.


The sample mean GPA of 80 UTM students is 3.12.


The standard deviation of grades for every student who has ever taken STA258.





1 - parameter (all NBA players) 2 - statistic (500 surveyed) 3 - statistic (80 students) 4 - parameter (every student ever)

title: STA258
text:
Compute a Statistic Yourself
Below are weekly study hours for a sample of 10 students. Calculate the sample mean.
















Solution


mean(study_hours)   # 19.6
This is \(\bar{x}\), our estimate of the unknown population mean \(\mu\).

title: STA258
text:
Descriptive vs Inferential Statistics

Descriptive statistics Numerical and graphical methods used to summarize the data you have.


Inferential statistics Using a sample to generalize to a larger population.

title: STA258
text:
Spot the Difference


Descriptive

“The average mark in this lecture of 80 students is 72%.”  “63% of these 400 Torontonians support the plan.”


Inferential

“We estimate the average STA258 mark across all sections is around 72%.”  “We estimate ~63% of all Toronto residents support the plan.”




Tell: descriptive describes this data. Inferential makes a claim beyond the data.

title: STA258
text:
1.2 Types of Data

Quantitative data Numerical measurements where arithmetic is meaningful.


Qualitative (categorical) data Values fall into categories; arithmetic doesn’t make sense.



Trap: a number doesn’t automatically mean quantitative. Postal codes, jersey numbers, and student IDs look numeric but are categorical.

title: STA258
text:
Quantitative: Discrete vs Continuous



Discrete Countable values, often whole numbers.



Number of siblings
Courses enrolled in
Defective items in a batch




Continuous Any value in a range; measured on a scale.



Height
Time spent studying
Temperature

title: STA258
text:
Qualitative: Nominal vs Ordinal



Nominal Categories with no natural order.



Eye colour
Program of study
Primary device (phone/laptop/tablet)




Ordinal Categories with a natural order.



Self-rated health (Poor → Excellent)
Movie rating (1–5 stars)
Education level

title: STA258
text:
The Full Map

                       DATA
                        |
          +-------------+-------------+
          |                           |
    Quantitative                 Qualitative
     (numeric)                  (categorical)
          |                           |
    +-----+------+              +-----+------+
    |            |              |            |
 Discrete    Continuous     Nominal       Ordinal
 (counts)    (measured)   (no order)    (ordered)



This tree is the single most useful diagram in Chapter 1.

title: STA258
text:
Classify These
Try classifying each one. Then click for the answer.







Variable
Type




Number of pets a student owns
Quantitative, discrete


Language spoken at home
Qualitative, nominal


Backpack weight (kg)
Quantitative, continuous


Postal code
Qualitative, nominal


Classroom temperature (°C)
Quantitative, continuous


Self-rated health (Poor/Fair/Good)
Qualitative, ordinal

title: STA258
text:
Check Data Types in R
R can tell you what type each variable is. Run it, then change a value.

title: STA258
text:
1.3 Introduction to Inferential Statistics

Sample → Statistic → Estimate of Parameter

title: STA258
text:
Why Uncertainty Is Built In

Two different samples of 100 UTM students will give two different sample means. So any estimate must come with a measure of how much it might be off.



This is where the tools in later chapters come from:

Confidence intervals - a range of plausible parameter values
Hypothesis tests - checking whether data support a specific claim
Statistical power - how likely we are to detect a real effect

title: STA258
text:
See Sampling Variability









Run it 5 times. Notice the sample mean changes each time - even though the true population mean is exactly 70.

title: STA258
text:
A Closer Look at Sampling
How you collect your sample determines whether inference is honest.

Simple random sample (SRS) - every unit has an equal chance of being chosen.


Systematic - every \(k\)-th unit from an ordered list (e.g., every 50th student).


Stratified - divide into groups, sample from each group.


Cluster - randomly pick whole groups, survey everyone inside.


Convenience / Voluntary response - whoever is easy to reach or chooses to respond. ⚠ Usually biased.

title: STA258
text:
Bias: When Samples Lie

Bias - a systematic tendency for a sample to differ from the population in a particular direction.




Classic bias traps:

Selection bias - surveying gym-goers about exercise habits
Voluntary response - Instagram polls (only loud opinions answer)
Non-response bias - the people who skip the survey are different from those who answer
Coverage bias - your sampling frame misses parts of the population

title: STA258
text:
Observational vs Experimental Studies



Observational Record what’s already happening - no intervention.


Survey adults: do you take vitamins? Did you catch a cold?



Experimental Randomly assign participants to treatments, then compare.


Randomly give half the volunteers Vitamin C, half a placebo, and compare cold rates.




Only well-designed experiments can establish cause and effect.

title: STA258
text:
Chapter 1 Summary



Statistics = science of learning from data under uncertainty.


We study a sample to learn about a population.


Parameters describe populations (unknown); statistics describe samples (known).


Data are quantitative (discrete/continuous) or qualitative (nominal/ordinal).


Inference uses a sample statistic to estimate an unknown population parameter - with a measure of uncertainty.


How we sample (and whether we experiment) determines whether inference is trustworthy.

title: STA258
text:
What’s Next

Chapter 2: we move into R - the tool we’ll use to compute statistics, simulate samples, and visualize uncertainty.



End of Chapter 1 ← Back to Chapter 1

title: STA258
text:
Bonus Practice
Below are some ages of UTM students in a sample. Compute:

The sample size \(n\)
The sample mean \(\bar{x}\)
The sample standard deviation \(s\)

















Solution


n    <- length(ages)
xbar <- mean(ages)
s    <- sd(ages)
\(n = 12\), \(\bar{x} \approx 20.83\), \(s \approx 1.59\).

title: STA258
text:
Why Learn R?

Every statistician on Earth uses code.You’re about to learn why.



Three reasons:

Reproducibility — every step is written down. Re-run it next year and get the same answer.
Scale — the same script that analyzes 10 rows also analyzes 10 million.
Sharing — send your code, not screenshots of your clicks.





This whole chapter is just enough R to do the rest of STA258. You don’t need to become a software engineer.

title: STA258
text:
Roadmap
2.1 The Statistical Computing Language

What R is and why we use it
Point-and-click vs code

2.2 Installing R and RStudio

R vs RStudio
Installing R
Installing RStudio
The RStudio panes and workflow
Positron


Sections 2.3 onward are coming soon.

title: STA258
text:
2.1 What is R?

R
R is used for data manipulation, statistics, and graphics. It supports a wide range of operations (+, −, /, <, etc.) on vectors, arrays, and matrices, and includes a large collection of built-in functions and user-contributed packages.




Free Open-source and free to install on any platform.


Vast 20,000+ contributed packages on CRAN.


Speaks Many Languages Interfaces with C, C++, FORTRAN, Python, and SQL.

title: STA258
text:
Point-and-click vs Code



Point-and-click software Quick at first. But every click is invisible — hard to repeat, hard to audit, hard to share.



R scripts Every step is written down. The script is the analysis — you can re-run it, version it, share it, or modify one line and re-run everything.




Bottom line: R makes statistical work reproducible, transparent, and automatic.

title: STA258
text:
2.2 R vs RStudio



R The programming language. The thing that does the calculations.



RStudio The interface. Where you write code, see plots, view files, and read output.




Analogy: R is the engine. RStudio is the dashboard. You can drive an engine without a dashboard — but you wouldn’t want to.




Install order matters: install R first, then RStudio on top.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.1 Installing R

Go to cran.r-project.org and pick your operating system.



Windows → Download R for Windows → base → run the installer
macOS → Download R for macOS → pick the matching .pkg file
Linux → follow your distribution’s instructions




Once installed, R can run from a terminal — but in this course we always use it through RStudio.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.2 Installing RStudio

RStudio An integrated development environment (IDE) designed for working with R.


RStudio gives you:

a script editor (write code)
a console (run code)
environment & history (see your objects)
files / plots / packages / help (everything else)



Mention: RStudio is now made by Posit. Same product, new company name.

title: STA258
text:
RStudio Panes


Source Write and save R scripts, R Markdown, and Quarto files here.


Console Run commands and see immediate output, messages, and errors.


Environment / History View objects currently in memory and commands that have been run.


Files / Plots / Packages / Help Manage files, view plots, load packages, and read help pages.





Write Type commands in the Source pane so the work is saved.


Run Cmd + Enter (Mac) or Ctrl + Enter (Windows).


Check Inspect objects, plots, and help pages using the other panes.

title: STA258
text:
A Typical Workflow


Open RStudio
Create a new R script (File → New File → R Script)
Type your code in the Source pane
Hit Cmd/Ctrl + Enter to run the current line
See results in the Console or Plots pane
Save the script so you can re-run later




Saving the script is the whole point — your analysis lives in the file, not in the console.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.3 Positron



What is Positron?
Positron is a cross-platform desktop IDE developed by Posit PBC (formerly RStudio). It is built on the Visual Studio Code architecture and powered by Electron, and runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
It supports R, Python, JavaScript, and Quarto documents.
Download: positron.posit.co




RStudio and Posit
RStudio is maintained by Posit, a company that also develops Shiny, Quarto, and R Markdown.



Positron is not required for this course. RStudio is the standard tool. Positron may become relevant in future workflows involving collaborative or browser-based coding.

title: STA258
text:
2.3 Programming in R
R was designed for:

data analysis
statistical calculations
visualization
reproducible reporting



Goal for this course: learn enough R to compute, summarize, visualize, and report data clearly. Nothing more.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.1 R as a Calculator

R handles arithmetic, exponents, logs, trig, and many other operations out of the box.




Operation
R Code
Result




Addition
2 + 3
5


Subtraction
4 - 1
3


Multiplication
6 * 2
12


Division
8 / 2
4


Exponentiation
5^2
25

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Basic Arithmetic
Edit any of the lines and run. Try 2 + 3 * 4 — what does R do first?

title: STA258
text:
Modulo and Integer Division



Operation
R Code
Meaning




Modulo
9 %% 4
Remainder after division


Integer division
9 %/% 4
Whole-number part of division













Modulo is surprisingly useful — checking if a number is even (x %% 2 == 0), wrapping indices, etc.

title: STA258
text:
Vectors

Vector A one-dimensional sequence of values, all of the same type. Built with c().











R applies operations element-by-element. No for loops needed for most arithmetic.

title: STA258
text:
Math Functions



Function
Meaning
Example




sqrt()
Square root
sqrt(16) → 4


log()
Natural log
log(10)


log10()
Log base 10
log10(100) → 2


exp()
Exponential
exp(1) → e


abs()
Absolute value
abs(-5) → 5


round()
Round to digits
round(3.1415, 2) → 3.14

title: STA258
text: Try It: Math Functions

title: STA258
text:
Summary Functions
The functions you’ll use most in this course:



Function
Returns




mean(x)
Average (\(\bar{x}\))


sum(x)
Total


min(x) / max(x)
Smallest / largest


sd(x)
Standard deviation (\(s\))


var(x)
Variance (\(s^2\))


length(x)
Sample size (\(n\))

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Summary Functions
Change the values in x, then re-run.

title: STA258
text:
Quick Practice: Calculate the Mean
Fill in the function name to compute the sample mean of x.
















Solution


mean(x)   # = 10.6
This is \(\bar{x}\).

title: STA258
text:
2.3.2 The Six Data Structures



Vectors — 1D, same type
Lists — flexible, mixed types
Matrices — 2D, same type



Arrays — 3D+, same type
Factors — categorical
Data frames — real datasets




You’ll meet all six this chapter — but in STA258 you’ll spend 80% of your time with vectors and data frames.

title: STA258
text:
Vectors













Numeric vector
A vector stores multiple values of the same type in one object. The function c() combines values.
mean() computes the average; length() counts the elements.

title: STA258
text:
Lists













List
A list stores elements of different types in one object - unlike a vector, which requires all elements to be the same type.
Lists are used to organise complex or mixed data, and are the building block for data frames.

title: STA258
text:
Matrices













Matrix
A matrix is a two-dimensional data structure storing elements of the same type. It supports addition, multiplication, transposition, and inversion.
Used in linear algebra, statistics, and data analysis.

title: STA258
text:
Matrix Operations










%*% is matrix multiplication. Plain * is element-wise — they are very different!

title: STA258
text:
Arrays

A multi-dimensional structure. You can think of a 3D array as a stack of matrices.











Arrays are rare in intro stats — included for completeness.

title: STA258
text:
Factors

A factor stores a categorical variable. R remembers which values are valid (the “levels”).











Factors are how R handles qualitative data — gender, education level, treatment group, etc.

title: STA258
text:
Reading CSV-Style Data













Data frame
A data frame is a table where each row is one observation and each column is one variable. Columns can hold different types.
read.csv() reads comma-separated data into a data frame.

title: STA258
text:
Cheat Sheet



Structure
Dimensions
Same type?
Use for




Vector
1D
Yes
A column of values


List
flexible
No
Mixed information


Matrix
2D
Yes
Numerical tables


Array
3D+
Yes
High-D data


Factor
1D
Yes
Categorical variable


Data frame
2D
No
Real datasets

title: STA258
text:
2.3.3 Functions

Most R commands look like function_name(input). The function takes inputs and returns output.











Common error: missing brackets, commas, or quotes. R is unforgiving.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.4 Histograms

Histogram A graph showing the distribution of a numerical variable by grouping values into intervals (bins).











faithful records eruption length and waiting time between eruptions.

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Make a Histogram










The shape (two humps!) tells you something real about the data — Old Faithful has two distinct eruption modes.

title: STA258
text:
Behind the Histogram
You can grab the numbers a histogram uses:










This is useful when you want exact counts for a report, or to compare two distributions.

title: STA258
text:
Practice: Change the Variable
Change the code below so it plots the histogram of eruptions instead of waiting.
















Solution


hist(faithful$eruptions)
You should see two clear peaks — short eruptions (~2 min) and long eruptions (~4.5 min).

title: STA258
text:
2.3.5 Installing and Loading Packages


# 1. Install once (downloads from CRAN)
install.packages("ggplot2")
install.packages("tidyverse")

# 2. Load every session
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)   # loads dplyr, tidyr,
                     # readr, purrr, and more

# View all installed packages
installed.packages()

# Search for packages by keyword
help.search("regression")



Packages
R packages provide specialised functions stored on CRAN (the Comprehensive R Archive Network), which you accessed when installing R.
Install a package once with install.packages(), then load it each session with library().


tidyverse
A meta-package that bundles ggplot2 (plots), dplyr (filter/select/mutate), tidyr, readr, purrr, and others. One install, one library call, the full data-science toolkit.

title: STA258
text: Try It: Inspect Packages

title: STA258
text:
Chapter 2 Summary


R Open-source language for statistics, graphics, and reproducible analysis. Interfaces with C, C++, and FORTRAN. Freely available from CRAN.


RStudio The main interface for writing and running R code. Four panes: Source, Console, Environment/History, Files/Plots/Help.


Data Structures Vector, list, matrix, array, factor, data frame. The data frame is the most common structure in applied statistics.


Indexing v[i] for vectors. df[row, col] for data frames. df$col to extract a column. R counts from 1.





Functions mean(), max(), min(), sort(), order(), hist(), names(), dim(), head(), summary()


Packages install.packages() once. library() every session. installed.packages() to list. help.search() to find.


ggplot2 ggplot() + aes() + geom_*() + labs() builds professional labelled plots.

title: STA258
text:
What’s Next

Chapter 3: we use these R tools to compute and visualize descriptive statistics — means, medians, spreads, and the shape of data.



End of Chapter 2 ← Back to Chapter 2

title: Chapter 2: An Introduction to R
text: Slides PDF

title: Chapter 2: An Introduction to R
text:
What you’ll learn
By the end of Chapter 2 you’ll be able to:

Explain why we use R rather than point-and-click software, and tell R apart from RStudio.
Install R and RStudio and navigate the four main panes of the IDE.
Use R as a calculator — arithmetic, exponents, logs, and built-in math functions.
Create and work with the core R data structures: vectors, lists, matrices, arrays, factors, and data frames.
Call built-in functions like mean(), sd(), hist() and read their output.
Install and load packages with install.packages() and library().

title: Chapter 2: An Introduction to R
text:
Key ideas

R vs RStudio
R is the engine that runs the calculations. RStudio is the dashboard you use to drive it. You install R first, then RStudio on top of it.


Scripts beat point-and-click
Writing analysis as code makes it reproducible, easy to share, and easy to re-run when the data changes. This is the single biggest reason statisticians use R.


R as a calculator
R handles + - * /, exponentiation ^, modulo %%, integer division %/%, and a full set of math functions (sqrt, log, exp, abs, round) and trig functions out of the box.


Data structures

Vectors — same-type values in one dimension
Lists — mixed types, any structure
Matrices / Arrays — same type, 2+ dimensions
Factors — categorical variables with named levels
Data frames — table of variables (columns) and observations (rows)



Functions and packages
Most R commands look like function_name(input). Packages extend R with new functions — install once with install.packages(), load each session with library().

title: Chapter 2: An Introduction to R
text:
How to use this chapter

Open the slides for an interactive walkthrough — every code block in the deck is editable and runs in your browser.
Use the PDF button for an offline-friendly version.
Try the in-slide exercises (mean, histogram). Change values, re-run, and check the output.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text: Slides PDF

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
What you’ll learn
By the end of Chapter 1 you’ll be able to:

Distinguish a population from a sample, and identify the unit, observation, and variables in a dataset.
Tell a parameter apart from a statistic — and know which is usually known.
Classify data as quantitative (discrete or continuous) or qualitative (nominal or ordinal).
Recognize common sampling methods and the biases they introduce.
Explain the goal of statistical inference: using a sample to learn about a population, with a measure of uncertainty.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
Key ideas

Statistics in one sentence
The science of learning from data under uncertainty — moving from what we measured to what we want to know.


Population vs Sample
A population is the full group we want to learn about. A sample is the subset we actually measure. We use the sample to make claims about the population.


Parameter vs Statistic
A parameter (e.g. \(\mu\), \(p\), \(\sigma\)) summarizes the population — usually unknown. A statistic (e.g. \(\bar{x}\), \(\hat{p}\), \(s\)) summarizes the sample — known, and used to estimate the parameter.


Types of Data
Quantitative data are numeric: discrete (counts) or continuous (measured). Qualitative data are categorical: nominal (no order) or ordinal (ordered).


Inference
We collect a sample, calculate a statistic, and use it to estimate an unknown population parameter — always with a measure of uncertainty. This sets up confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and the rest of the course.

title: Chapter 1: Introduction
text:
How to use this chapter

Open the slides above for the full walkthrough, with interactive R code blocks you can edit and run in your browser.
Use the PDF button for an offline-friendly version (interactive elements won’t run there).
Try the practice exercises from the textbook at the end of Chapter 1.

title: STA258 Slides
text:
Resources

STA258 e-book

title: STA258
text:
Roadmap


1. Setup Install R, install RStudio, understand CRAN, and identify the main RStudio panes.


2. R basics Use R as a calculator, store values in variables, create vectors, and use basic functions.


3. Data work Read data frames, select rows and columns, make histograms, and create plots with ggplot2.




Design rule for this deck: each WebR coding slide is self-contained, so students can jump around without depending on objects created on earlier slides.

title: STA258
text:
2.1 The Statistical Computing Language



R
R is used for data manipulation, statistics, and graphics. It supports a wide range of operations (+, −, /, <, etc.) on vectors, arrays, and matrices, and includes a large collection of built-in functions and user-contributed packages.



Interoperability R can interface with C, C++, FORTRAN, and other languages. It is open-source and freely available from CRAN (the Comprehensive R Archive Network).




Point-and-click vs scripting
Point-and-click tools are useful for quick work, but the steps behind the result can be hard to review or repeat.



Why scripts matter
R lets us write scripts that document each step of an analysis. This promotes reproducibility, version control, and transparency — analyses can be automated, customised, and rerun on new data with minimal effort.

title: STA258
text:
Why Use R?



Point-and-click software Often easier at first, but steps may be harder to document and repeat.



R scripts Each step is written down, so the analysis is easier to check, repeat, and modify.




Main idea: R makes statistical work reproducible, transparent, and easier to automate.

title: STA258
text:
2.2 Installing R and RStudio


R
The programming language that performs the calculations.
Install this first.


RStudio
The interface for writing scripts, running code, viewing plots, and managing files.
Install this second.





Step 1 Download R from CRAN.


Step 2 Install RStudio Desktop from Posit.


Step 3 Open RStudio and confirm the Console panel appears.




RStudio is the interface but R does the computation — R must be installed first.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.1 Installing R

Step 1 Go to the official R project website: cran.r-project.org



Choose your operating system:


Windows: Download R for Windows → base → download the installer


macOS: Download R for macOS → choose the correct .pkg file


Linux: Follow the distribution-specific instructions




After installation, R can be accessed from a terminal or through RStudio.

title: STA258
text:
Installing R: Teaching Note

Important: Students should install R before installing RStudio, because RStudio needs an R installation to run code.



The ebook screenshot shows the CRAN page where students choose their operating system and download the correct installer.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.2 Installing RStudio


Open RStudio Download

Install RStudio Desktop:

Open the Posit download page.
Choose RStudio Desktop (free).
Download the installer for your OS.
Run the installer.
Open RStudio and check the Console.





No embedded preview
The Posit page blocks embedded frames. Use the button above to open the official page.



After installation
RStudio auto-detects R if R was installed first. If the Console shows an error, reinstall R and then reopen RStudio.

title: STA258
text:
RStudio Workflow


1. Write Type commands in the Source pane so your work is saved.


2. Run Send code to the Console to execute it.


3. Check Use the Environment, Plots, Files, and Help tabs to inspect results.




A good habit is to keep important work in a script instead of only typing commands directly into the Console.

title: STA258
text:
The Four Main RStudio Panes







Pane
Purpose




Source
Write and edit scripts or Quarto files


Console
Run commands and view immediate output


Environment / History
View saved objects and past commands


Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer
Manage files, plots, packages, and documentation





The ebook shows the RStudio home screen and the layout after opening a new script.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.3 Positron



What is Positron?
Positron is a cross-platform desktop IDE developed by Posit PBC (formerly RStudio). It is built on the Visual Studio Code architecture and powered by Electron, and runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux.
It supports R, Python, JavaScript, and Quarto documents.
Download: positron.posit.co




RStudio and Posit
RStudio is maintained by Posit, a company that also develops Shiny, Quarto, and R Markdown.



Positron is not required for this course. RStudio is the standard tool. Positron may become relevant in future workflows involving collaborative or browser-based coding.

title: STA258
text:
2.3 Programming in R

Goal of this section Use WebR to learn R basics directly inside the slide deck.


We will practice:

R as a calculator
declaring variables
declaring numeric vectors
indexing vectors
using data frames
isolating rows and columns
loading packages

title: STA258
text:
2.3.1 R as a Powerful Calculator

R can be used like a calculator. It supports arithmetic, exponentiation, logarithms, trigonometric functions, and summary calculations.





Operation
R Code
Result




Addition
2 + 3
5


Subtraction
4 - 1
3


Multiplication
6 * 2
12


Division
8 / 2
4


Exponentiation
5^2
25

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Basic Arithmetic
Edit the numbers and run the code.

title: STA258
text:
More Arithmetic Operators



Operation
R Code
Meaning




Modulo
9 %% 4
Remainder after division


Integer division
9 %/% 4
Whole-number part of division

title: STA258
text:
Vector Operations

Vector A vector is a one-dimensional collection of values of the same type.












Operations on vectors are performed element by element.

title: STA258
text:
Math Functions in R
R has many built-in mathematical functions.



Function
Meaning
Example




sqrt()
Square root
sqrt(16)


log()
Natural log
log(10)


log10()
Log base 10
log10(100)


exp()
Exponential function
exp(1)


abs()
Absolute value
abs(-5)


round()
Round to decimal places
round(3.1415, 2)

title: STA258
text: Try It: Math Functions

title: STA258
text:
Summary Functions
Common functions for summarizing data:



Function
Meaning




mean(x)
Average


sum(x)
Total


min(x)
Minimum


max(x)
Maximum


sd(x)
Standard deviation


var(x)
Variance

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Summary Functions
Change the values in x, then rerun the code.

title: STA258
text:
Quick Practice: Calculate the Mean
Fill in the blank to calculate the mean.
















Solution


mean(x)

title: STA258
text:
2.3.2 Basic R Data Structures
The primary data structures introduced in the ebook are:



Vectors
Lists
Matrices



Arrays
Factors
Data frames




Main idea: Different data structures organize information in different ways.

title: STA258
text:
Vectors













Numeric vector
A vector stores multiple values of the same type in one object. The function c() combines values.
mean() computes the average; length() counts the elements.

title: STA258
text:
Lists













List
A list stores elements of different types in one object — unlike a vector, which requires all elements to be the same type.
Lists are used to organise complex or mixed data, and are the building block for data frames.

title: STA258
text:
Matrices













Matrix
A matrix is a two-dimensional data structure storing elements of the same type. It supports addition, multiplication, transposition, and inversion.
Used in linear algebra, statistics, and data analysis.

title: STA258
text:
Matrix Operations










The operator %*% is used for matrix multiplication. t() transposes a matrix.

title: STA258
text:
Arrays

Array A multi-dimensional structure that can have more than two dimensions.

title: STA258
text:
Factors

Factor A data structure used to represent categorical variables.












Factors are useful for categorical data such as gender, education level, or survey responses.

title: STA258
text:
Reading CSV-Style Data













Data frame
A data frame is a table where each row is one observation and each column is one variable. Columns can hold different types.
read.csv() reads comma-separated data into a data frame.

title: STA258
text:
Data Structure Summary



Structure
Shape
Best Used For




Vector
One dimension
Values of the same type


List
Flexible
Mixed types or grouped information


Matrix
Two dimensions
Same-type numerical tables


Array
Multiple dimensions
Higher-dimensional data


Factor
One dimension
Categorical variables


Data frame
Rows and columns
Real datasets

title: STA258
text:
2.3.3 Using Basic Functions in R
R has many built-in functions that help calculate, transform, and summarize data.

title: STA258
text:
Function Logic

General form Most R functions use the structure function_name(input).



For example, mean(x) tells R to apply the function mean to the object x.



Common mistake: Forgetting brackets, commas, or quotation marks will cause R to return an error.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.4 Make a Histogram Using RStudio












What the histogram shows
A histogram groups numeric values into intervals and shows how many observations fall in each interval.
Here, it shows the distribution of waiting times in the faithful dataset.

title: STA258
text:
Try It: Create a Histogram
Run the code to create a histogram of waiting times in the faithful dataset.

title: STA258
text:
Histogram Breaks and Counts
The ebook also shows how to access the numerical values behind a histogram.










breaks show the interval endpoints. counts show how many observations fall in each interval.

title: STA258
text:
Quick Practice: Histogram Variable
Change the code so it creates a histogram of eruptions instead of waiting.
















Solution


hist(faithful$eruptions)

title: STA258
text:
2.3.5 Installing and Loading Packages

Package A package is a collection of extra functions, data, and tools that extend what R can do.



Install a package once:
install.packages("ggplot2")
Load it every new R session:
library(ggplot2)

title: STA258
text:
Package Reminder

Important: You install a package only once, but you must load it with library() every time you restart RStudio.

title: STA258
text:
Chapter 2 Summary


R Open-source language for statistics, graphics, and reproducible analysis. Interfaces with C, C++, and FORTRAN. Freely available from CRAN.


RStudio The main interface for writing and running R code. Four panes: Source, Console, Environment/History, Files/Plots/Help.


Data Structures Vector, list, matrix, array, factor, data frame. The data frame is the most common structure in applied statistics.


Indexing v[i] for vectors. df[row, col] for data frames. df$col to extract a column. R counts from 1.





Functions mean(), max(), min(), sort(), order(), hist(), names(), dim(), head(), summary()


Packages install.packages() once. library() every session. installed.packages() to list. help.search() to find.


ggplot2 ggplot() + aes() + geom_*() + labs() builds professional labelled plots.

title: STA258
text:
End of Chapter 2

End of Chapter 2

Back to Home

title: STA258
text: Work in Progress

title: STA258
text:
Install R and RStudio

Before coding in RStudio, students need both R and RStudio.





R The programming language that performs the calculations.



RStudio The interface where students write scripts, run code, and view output.




Order: Install R first, then install RStudio.

title: STA258
text:
Live Install Guide

Open CRAN: Install R
Open Posit: Install RStudio



Note: Some official websites block embedding inside slides. If the live page does not load below, use the buttons above.

title: STA258
text:
RStudio: What Students Should Recognize
After installation, students should recognize four main areas:







Pane
Purpose




Source
write scripts and Quarto files


Console
run code and see immediate output


Environment / History
view saved objects and past commands


Files / Plots / Packages / Help / Viewer
manage files, plots, packages, help pages, and previews




Important shortcut: Use Command + Enter on Mac or Ctrl + Enter on Windows to run a line of code.

title: STA258
text:
What WebR Does

WebR WebR lets students run R code directly inside the HTML slide deck.



Students can edit code, run it, make mistakes, fix them, and rerun the code without leaving the slides.



Goal for this chapter: Learn R basics by doing them live.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.1 R as a Powerful Calculator















Symbol
Meaning




+
Add


-
Subtract


*
Multiply


/
Divide


^
Power




R follows standard order of operations. Use parentheses to control evaluation.

title: STA258
text:
Calculator Practice


Task Edit the code to calculate:
(12 + 8) / 5

Expected result: 4


















Solution


(12 + 8) / 5

title: STA258
text:
More Operators













9 %% 4 → remainder after dividing 9 by 4.
9 %/% 4 → how many complete 4s fit into 9.
sqrt(), round(), and abs() take inputs inside parentheses — these are function calls.

title: STA258
text:
Declaring Variables













Variable
A variable stores a value so it can be reused later. The assignment arrow <- means “store the value on the right in the name on the left.”

title: STA258
text:
Variable Practice


Task Create a variable called mark storing the value 87, then display it.


















Solution


mark <- 87

mark

title: STA258
text:
Changing Variables
Run the code once. Then change hours and run it again.









Main idea: If the stored values change, the result changes too.

title: STA258
text:
Vector Practice


Task Create a vector temps with:
21, 23, 20, 25, 24
Then compute its mean.


















Solution


temps <- c(21, 23, 20, 25, 24)

mean(temps)

title: STA258
text:
Indexing from a Vector













Indexing
Square brackets [ ] select elements by position.
R starts counting at 1, not 0.
2:4 creates the sequence 2, 3, 4.

title: STA258
text:
Vector Indexing Practice


Task Extract the second and fourth values from marks.


















Solution


marks <- c(80, 75, 90, 88, 92)

marks[c(2, 4)]

title: STA258
text:
Isolating Columns
Use $ to select one column from a data frame.









class_data$mark means “take the mark column from class_data.”

title: STA258
text:
Column Practice


Task Find the average number of study hours.


















Solution


csv_text <- "student,mark,hours
A,80,6
B,75,5
C,90,8
D,88,7"

class_data <- read.csv(text = csv_text)

mean(class_data$hours)

title: STA258
text:
Isolating Rows and Columns
Use square brackets:
[ ]

title: STA258
text:
Row and Column Practice


Task Select the first two rows and only the student and mark columns.


















Solution


csv_text <- "student,mark,hours
A,80,6
B,75,5
C,90,8
D,88,7"

class_data <- read.csv(text = csv_text)

class_data[1:2, c("student", "mark")]

title: STA258
text:
Reading a CSV File
In normal RStudio, we can read a CSV file using:
my_data <- read.csv("my_file.csv")


In WebR: Local file reading is harder because the code runs in the browser. For this slide set, we use built-in datasets that already behave like data frames.

title: STA258
text:
Built-in Dataset Practice
Use the built-in dataset faithful.

title: STA258
text: Histogram from a Data Frame

title: STA258
text:
Histogram Practice


Task Change the histogram to show eruption duration instead of waiting time. Update the title and x-axis label.


















Solution


data(faithful)

hist(
  faithful$eruptions,
  main   = "Duration of Old Faithful Eruptions",
  xlab   = "Eruption duration (minutes)",
  col    = "lightblue",
  border = "white"
)

title: STA258
text:
2.3.5 Installing and Loading Packages


# 1. Install once (downloads from CRAN)
install.packages("ggplot2")

# 2. Load every session
library(ggplot2)

# View all installed packages
installed.packages()

# Search for packages by keyword
help.search("regression")



Packages
R packages provide specialised functions. They are stored on CRAN (the Comprehensive R Archive Network), which you accessed when installing R. GitHub-hosted packages also exist for advanced users.
You only need to install a package once, but you must load it with library() every time you restart RStudio.





In WebR: packages listed in the YAML are pre-loaded for browser use — no install.packages() call needed here.

title: STA258
text:
Using ggplot2 in WebR












Code structure
ggplot() starts the plot.
aes() maps variables to axes.
geom_point() chooses the plot type.
labs() adds professional labels.

title: STA258
text:
ggplot2 Practice


Task Change the plot so the x-axis shows student and the y-axis shows mark, using a bar chart instead of points.
Hint: replace geom_point() with geom_col().


















Solution


library(ggplot2)

csv_text <- "student,mark,hours
A,80,6
B,75,5
C,90,8
D,88,7"

class_data <- read.csv(text = csv_text)

ggplot(class_data, aes(x = student, y = mark)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#00695c") +
  labs(
    title = "Marks by Student",
    x     = "Student",
    y     = "Mark"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

title: STA258
text:
2.2.1 Installing R: Live Page

 Open CRAN in New Tab

Download and run the installer.


Use the default installation settings.

text:

 Open RStudio Desktop Download

title: STA258
text:
RStudio Panes


Source Write and save R scripts, R Markdown, and Quarto files here.


Console Run commands and see immediate output, messages, and errors.


Environment / History View objects currently in memory and commands that have been run.


Files / Plots / Packages / Help Manage files, view plots, load packages, and read help pages.





Write Type commands in the Source pane so the work is saved.


Run Cmd + Enter (Mac) or Ctrl + Enter (Windows).


Check Inspect objects, plots, and help pages using the other panes.

title: STA258
text: Open CRAN in New Tab

title: STA258
text:
2.2.1 Installing R: Live CRAN Page


 Open CRAN in New Tab 

Install R:


Open CRAN.


Choose Windows, macOS, or Linux.


Download the installer.


Use the default installation settings.




If the embedded page is hard to use, open the official site with the button.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.2 Installing RStudio: Live Posit Page


 Open RStudio Download 

Install RStudio:


Open the Posit download page.


Download RStudio Desktop.


Run the installer.


Open RStudio and check the Console.




RStudio should automatically detect the R installation.

title: STA258
section: If a Live Page Does Not Load
text:
If a Live Page Does Not Load

Important: Some websites block embedded pages inside slide decks. If a page is blank or too small to use, open the official site with the green button instead.



Teaching use: The slide still works because the button sends students to the current official website.

title: STA258
text:
2.3 Introduction to Programming in R


Calculator Use R for arithmetic, powers, roots, rounding, and functions.


Objects Store values in variables and reuse them in later commands.


Data Structures Work with vectors, lists, matrices, arrays, factors, and data frames.


Selection Use indexing to select elements, rows, and columns.


Plots Make histograms and simple graphics from data.


Packages Load extra tools such as ggplot2.

title: STA258
text:
Why Scripts Matter



Point-and-click workflow Useful for quick work, but the exact steps can be harder to document, repeat, or check.



Script workflow Each step is written down, so the analysis can be rerun, edited, and shared.




This is why R is useful for reproducible statistics and academic research.

title: STA258
text:
Data Frame Basics















Command
What it does




head()
View first rows


dim()
Count rows and columns


names()
List column names


summary()
Summary statistics




Run these checks whenever you open a new dataset.

title: STA258
text:
Built-In Dataset: faithful












faithful
A built-in R dataset about the Old Faithful geyser.
It has two numeric variables: eruption duration and waiting time between eruptions.

title: STA258
text:
Scripts Make Statistics Reproducible


Point-and-click workflow
Useful for quick work, but the exact steps can be difficult to document, repeat, or debug.
Risk: the result may be hard for another person to verify.


Script workflow
Every command is written down, so the analysis can be rerun, edited, checked, and shared.
Benefit: the same code can be used on new datasets.




Main idea: scripts support reproducibility, version control, and transparency in data analysis.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.1 Installing R: CRAN Page


Open CRAN in New Tab

Install R:

Open CRAN.
Choose Windows, macOS, or Linux.
Download the installer.
Use the default installation settings.



The embedded page is included because CRAN usually renders inside the slide deck. If it does not load, use the button.

title: STA258
text:
More Calculator Tools












Read the code
9 %% 4 asks for the remainder after dividing 9 by 4.
9 %/% 4 asks how many complete 4s fit into 9.
Functions such as sqrt(), round(), and abs() take inputs inside parentheses.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.2 Basic R Data Structures
The primary data structures in R are vectors, lists, matrices, arrays, factors, and data frames.



Structure
Stores
Dimensions
Types mixed?




Vector
One type of value
1-D
No


List
Any types
1-D
Yes


Matrix
One type of value
2-D
No


Array
One type of value
n-D
No


Factor
Categorical values
1-D
No


Data frame
Any types per column
2-D
Yes




For most applied statistics the main structure is the data frame, because it resembles a spreadsheet where each column can hold a different type.

title: STA258
text: Lists and Matrices

title: STA258
text:
Arrays, Factors, and Data Frames


Array
Multi-dimensional values of the same type.
array(1:12, dim = c(2, 3, 2))


Factor
Categorical values with levels.
factor(c("A", "B", "A"))


Data frame
A table where columns can have different types.
data.frame(name, mark)

title: STA258
text:
Isolating Columns and Rows













Two selection methods
data$column — select a column by name (returns a vector).
data[row, column] — select rows and columns by position or name.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.3 Using Basic Functions in R















Function
Purpose




max(), min()
Largest and smallest values


mean(), sum()
Average and total


sort()
Sorted values


order()
Rank positions




These functions work on vectors, and many also apply to data-frame columns or matrices with additional arguments.

title: STA258
text:
Histogram Components













plot = FALSE suppresses the graph and returns the underlying values — useful for checking breakpoints and counts directly.

title: STA258
text:
Chapter Roadmap


1 — Setup Install R and RStudio, understand the four RStudio panes, and learn about Positron.


2 — R Basics Use R as a calculator, create variables, build vectors, and apply common built-in functions.


3 — Data Work Read data frames, select rows and columns, make histograms, and use ggplot2.





Core skill Writing commands that make statistical work clear and repeatable.


Core tool RStudio — scripts, output, plots, and help pages in one place.


Core habit Save work in scripts rather than relying on the Console alone.

title: STA258
text:
R, Packages, and Reproducible Work


Language R gives us the commands used to calculate, transform, summarize, and visualize data.


Packages Packages are collections of extra functions, datasets, and tools that extend what R can do.


Scripts Scripts store commands in order, making an analysis easier to repeat, edit, check, and share.




Textbook connection: instead of only clicking through menus, students learn to write commands that document each step of the analysis.

title: STA258
text:
Calculator Practice: Solution


Solution idea Parentheses make R add 12 + 8 first. Then R divides the result by 5.
The calculation is:
(12 + 8) / 5 = 4


(12 + 8) / 5

title: STA258
text:
Variable Practice: Solution


Solution idea Use <- to assign the value 87 to the object name mark.
Then type mark to print the stored value.


mark <- 87

mark

title: STA258
text:
Vector Practice: Solution


Solution idea Use c() to combine the five numbers into one vector.
Then use mean(temps) to calculate the average.


temps <- c(21, 23, 20, 25, 24)

mean(temps)

title: STA258
text:
Vector Indexing Practice: Solution


Solution idea The second value is selected with 2, and the fourth value is selected with 4.
Use c(2, 4) because we want two positions at the same time.


marks <- c(80, 75, 90, 88, 92)

marks[c(2, 4)]

title: STA258
text:
Column Practice: Solution


Solution idea The column we need is hours, not mark.
Use $hours to select the study-hours column.


csv_text <- "student,mark,hours
A,80,6
B,75,5
C,90,8
D,88,7"

class_data <- read.csv(text = csv_text)

mean(class_data$hours)

title: STA258
text:
Row and Column Practice: Solution


Solution idea Rows 1:2 select the first two students.
Columns c("student", "mark") keep only the student name and mark columns.


csv_text <- "student,mark,hours
A,80,6
B,75,5
C,90,8
D,88,7"

class_data <- read.csv(text = csv_text)

class_data[1:2, c("student", "mark")]

title: STA258
text:
Histogram Practice: Solution


Solution idea Use faithful$eruptions instead of faithful$waiting.
Update the title and axis label so the plot matches the new variable.


data(faithful)

hist(
  faithful$eruptions,
  main = "Duration of Old Faithful Eruptions",
  xlab = "Eruption duration in minutes",
  col = "lightblue",
  border = "white"
)

title: STA258
text:
ggplot2 Practice: Solution


Solution idea Map student to the x-axis and mark to the y-axis.
Use geom_col() because each student should have one bar.


library(ggplot2)

csv_text <- "student,mark,hours
A,80,6
B,75,5
C,90,8
D,88,7"

class_data <- read.csv(text = csv_text)

ggplot(class_data, aes(x = student, y = mark)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#00695c") +
  labs(
    title = "Marks by Student",
    x = "Student",
    y = "Mark"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

title: STA258
text:
2.2.1 Installing R: CRAN


Open CRAN in New Tab

Install R:

Open CRAN.
Choose Windows, macOS, or Linux.
Download the installer.
Use the default installation settings.



If the page below does not load, use the button to open the official site directly.

title: STA258
section: 2.3 An Introduction to Programming in R
text:
2.3 An Introduction to Programming in R



Why R for statistics?
R is specifically designed for data analysis, statistics, and visualization. It offers a rich ecosystem of packages and built-in functions that make complex statistical tasks straightforward.
R is especially popular in academia due to its open-source nature, strong support for reproducible reporting, and integration with tools like R Markdown and Shiny.






Topic
Slides cover




Calculator
Arithmetic, operators, functions


Objects
Variables and assignment


Data structures
Vector, list, matrix, array, factor, data frame


Selection
Indexing rows and columns


Plots
Histograms and ggplot2


Packages
Install, load, search

title: STA258
text:
Arrays and Factors

























Arrays extend matrices to n dimensions. Useful for simulations and higher-dimensional data.


Factors store unique values (called levels) efficiently. Automatically treated as categorical in models.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.4 Histogram: the faithful Dataset













faithful
A built-in R dataset recording Old Faithful geyser eruptions. It has two numeric variables: eruption duration and waiting time between eruptions.



A histogram groups values into intervals and shows how many observations fall in each interval.

title: STA258
text:
Using ggplot2













ggplot2 structure
ggplot() — start the plot.
aes() — map variables to axes.
geom_point() — choose the plot type.
labs() — add labels.
theme_minimal() — clean background.

title: STA258
text:
Chapter Roadmap


1 - Setup Install R and RStudio, understand the four RStudio panes, and learn about Positron.


2 - R Basics Use R as a calculator, create variables, build vectors, and apply common built-in functions.


3 - Data Work Read data frames, select rows and columns, make histograms, and use ggplot2.





Core skill Writing commands that make statistical work clear and repeatable.


Core tool RStudio - scripts, output, plots, and help pages in one place.


Core habit Save work in scripts rather than relying on the Console alone.

title: STA258
text:
2.2 Installing R and RStudio


R
The programming language that performs the calculations.
Install this first.


RStudio
The interface for writing scripts, running code, viewing plots, and managing files.
Install this second.




RStudio is the interface but R does the computation - R must be installed first. The next two slides walk through each install.

title: STA258
text:
2.2.1 Installing R: CRAN



Install R:

Open CRAN.
Choose Windows, macOS, or Linux.
Download the installer.
Use the default installation settings.



If the page below does not load, use the button to open the official site directly.

Open CRAN in New Tab

title: STA258
text:
2.2.2 Installing RStudio



Install RStudio Desktop:

Open the Posit download page.
Choose RStudio Desktop (free).
Download the installer for your OS.
Run the installer.
Open RStudio and check the Console.



RStudio auto-detects R if R was installed first.

Open RStudio Download

title: STA258
section: 2.3 An Introduction to Programming in R
text:
2.3 An Introduction to Programming in R
Why R for statistics?

Built specifically for data analysis, statistics, and visualization
Rich ecosystem of packages and built-in statistical functions
Open source and free on every major operating system
Strong support for reproducible reporting through R Markdown and Quarto
Widely used in academia, industry, and government

title: STA258
text:
2.3.1 R as a Powerful Calculator















Symbol
Meaning




+
Add


-
Subtract


*
Multiply


/
Divide


^
Power




R follows standard order of operations. Use parentheses to control evaluation.

title: STA258
text:
More Operators













9 %% 4 → remainder after dividing 9 by 4.
9 %/% 4 → how many complete 4s fit into 9.
sqrt(), round(), and abs() take inputs inside parentheses - these are function calls.

title: STA258
text:
Calculator Practice


Task Translate this math expression into R and evaluate it:
(12 + 8) ÷ 5

Then try: 34 − √25


















Solution


(12 + 8) / 5
3^4 - sqrt(25)

title: STA258
text:
Declaring Variables













Variable
A variable stores a value so it can be reused later. The assignment arrow <- means “store the value on the right in the name on the left.”

title: STA258
text:
Variable Practice


Task Create a variable called mark storing the value 87, then display it.


















Solution


mark <- 87

mark

title: STA258
text:
2.3.2 Basic R Data Structures
The primary data structures in R are vectors, lists, matrices, arrays, factors, and data frames.



Structure
Stores
Dimensions
Types mixed?




Vector
One type of value
1-D
No


List
Any types
1-D
Yes


Matrix
One type of value
2-D
No


Array
One type of value
n-D
No


Factor
Categorical values
1-D
No


Data frame
Any types per column
2-D
Yes




For most applied statistics the main structure is the data frame, because it resembles a spreadsheet where each column can hold a different type.

title: STA258
text:
Vector Practice


Task Create a vector temps with:
21, 23, 20, 25, 24
Then compute its mean.


















Solution


temps <- c(21, 23, 20, 25, 24)

mean(temps)

title: STA258
text:
Indexing from a Vector













Indexing
Square brackets [ ] select elements by position.
R starts counting at 1, not 0.
2:4 creates the sequence 2, 3, 4.

title: STA258
text:
Vector Indexing Practice


Task Extract the second and fourth values from marks.


















Solution


marks <- c(80, 75, 90, 88, 92)

marks[c(2, 4)]

title: STA258
text:
Arrays and Factors

























Arrays extend matrices to n dimensions. Useful for simulations and higher-dimensional data.


Factors store unique values (called levels) efficiently. Automatically treated as categorical in models.

title: STA258
text:
Data Frame Basics















Command
What it does




head()
View first rows


dim()
Count rows and columns


names()
List column names


summary()
Summary statistics




Run these checks whenever you open a new dataset.

title: STA258
text:
Isolating Columns and Rows













Two selection methods
data$column - select a column by name (returns a vector).
data[row, column] - select rows and columns by position or name.

title: STA258
text:
Column Practice


Task Find the average number of study hours.


















Solution


csv_text <- "student,mark,hours
A,80,6
B,75,5
C,90,8
D,88,7"

class_data <- read.csv(text = csv_text)

mean(class_data$hours)

title: STA258
text:
Row and Column Practice


Task Select the first two rows and only the student and mark columns.


















Solution


csv_text <- "student,mark,hours
A,80,6
B,75,5
C,90,8
D,88,7"

class_data <- read.csv(text = csv_text)

class_data[1:2, c("student", "mark")]

title: STA258
text:
2.3.3 Using Basic Functions in R















Function
Purpose




max(), min()
Largest and smallest values


mean(), sum()
Average and total


sort()
Sorted values


order()
Rank positions




These functions work on vectors, and many also apply to data-frame columns or matrices with additional arguments.

title: STA258
text:
2.3.4 Histogram: the faithful Dataset













faithful
A built-in R dataset recording Old Faithful geyser eruptions. It has two numeric variables: eruption duration and waiting time between eruptions.



A histogram groups values into intervals and shows how many observations fall in each interval.

title: STA258
text:
Histogram Components













plot = FALSE suppresses the graph and returns the underlying values - useful for checking breakpoints and counts directly.

title: STA258
text:
Histogram Practice


Task Change the histogram to show eruption duration instead of waiting time. Update the title and x-axis label.


















Solution


data(faithful)

hist(
  faithful$eruptions,
  main   = "Duration of Old Faithful Eruptions",
  xlab   = "Eruption duration (minutes)",
  col    = "lightblue",
  border = "white"
)

title: STA258
text:
Using ggplot2













ggplot2 structure
ggplot() - start the plot.
aes() - map variables to axes.
geom_point() - choose the plot type.
labs() - add labels.
theme_minimal() - clean background.

title: STA258
text:
ggplot2 Practice


Task Change the plot so the x-axis shows student and the y-axis shows mark, using a bar chart instead of points.
Hint: replace geom_point() with geom_col().


















Solution


library(ggplot2)

csv_text <- "student,mark,hours
A,80,6
B,75,5
C,90,8
D,88,7"

class_data <- read.csv(text = csv_text)

ggplot(class_data, aes(x = student, y = mark)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#00695c") +
  labs(
    title = "Marks by Student",
    x     = "Student",
    y     = "Mark"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)

title: STA258
text:
End of Chapter 2

End of Chapter 2

Back to Home

title: STA258
text:
2.1 Why Scripts, Not Clicks?

Point-and-click vs scripting
Point-and-click tools are useful for quick work, but the steps behind the result can be hard to review or repeat.




Click workflow Open menu → choose options → click Run. Six months later: which options did you pick?


Script workflow mean(marks) saved to a .R file. Re-run on new data with one keystroke.




Why scripts matter
R lets us write scripts that document each step of an analysis. This promotes reproducibility, version control, and transparency - analyses can be automated, customised, and rerun on new data with minimal effort.

title: STA258
text:
Using tidyverse: dplyr













dplyr verbs
filter() - keep rows that match a condition.
select() - keep only the columns you name.
arrange() - sort rows by one or more columns.
The pipe |> (or %>%) chains verbs together: read it as “then”.
